merged_events.event_description: Diplomatic relations established with the Federation of Central American States when President Monroe received Antonio José Cañaz as Envoy Extraordinary and Minister Plenipotentiary. No resident mission yet established.

evidence.quote: Diplomatic relations were established on August 4, 1824, when President James Monroe received Antonio José Cañaz as Envoy Extraordinary and Minister Plenipotentiary on August 4, 1824.

merged_events.event_description: American Legation established in Guatemala when Chargé d'Affaires John Williams presented his credentials to the Federation of Central American States.

evidence.quote: Chargé d'Affaires John Williams presented his credentials to the Federation of Central American States on May 3, 1826.

merged_events.event_description: Diplomatic relations re-established with independent Guatemala when Chargé d'Affaires Elijah Hise presented his credentials to the Republic of Guatemala.

evidence.quote: Chargé d'Affaires Elijah Hise presented his credentials to the Republic of Guatemala on or shortly before January 21, 1849.

merged_events.event_description: American Legation in Guatemala raised to Embassy status when Henry C. Hall presented his credentials as Envoy Extraordinary and Minister Plenipotentiary.

evidence.quote: The American Legation in Guatemala was raised to Embassy status when Henry C. Hall, Envoy Extraordinary and Minister Plenipotentiary, presented his credentials on August 10, 1882.

merged_events.event_description: John Williams made farewell calls, ending the first U.S. mission to the Federation of the Centre of America. No successor was appointed until 1833.

evidence.quote: John Williams, Chargé d'Affaires. Appointed: 1825-12-29. Credentials presented: 1826-05-03. Made farewell calls 1826-12-01. Note: Commissioned to the Federation of the Centre of America.

merged_events.event_description: U.S. mission re-established to the Republic of Central America, with Witt Charles Gerrit De presenting credentials as Chargé d'Affaires.

evidence.quote: Witt Charles Gerrit De, Chargé d'Affaires. Appointed: 1833-01-29. Credentials presented: 1833-12-17. Left post about 1839-01-01. Note: Commissioned to the Republic of Central of America.

merged_events.event_description: Witt Charles Gerrit De left post, ending the U.S. mission to the Republic of Central America. No successor was appointed until 1848.

evidence.quote: Witt Charles Gerrit De, Chargé d'Affaires. Appointed: 1833-01-29. Credentials presented: 1833-12-17. Left post about 1839-01-01. Note: Commissioned to the Republic of Central of America.

merged_events.event_description: U.S. mission re-established to the Republic of Guatemala, with Elijah Hise presenting credentials as Chargé d'Affaires.

evidence.quote: Elijah Hise, Chargé d'Affaires. Appointed: 1848-03-31. Credentials presented: 1849-01-31. Presented recall about 1849-06-23. Note: Commissioned to the Republic of Guatemala.

merged_events.event_description: Normal diplomatic relations interrupted when the new government of Guatemala was unrecognized by the United States; McMillin left post on January 5, 1922.

evidence.quote: Benton Mcmillin, Envoy Extraordinary and Minister Plenipotentiary. Appointed: 1919-09-23. Credentials presented: 1920-01-15. Normal relations interrupted on 1921-12-06. Note: The new government of Guatemala was still unrecognized by the United States when McMillin left post on January 5, 1922.

merged_events.event_description: U.S. Legation re-established in Guatemala with Arthur H Geissler presenting credentials as Envoy Extraordinary and Minister Plenipotentiary, restoring normal diplomatic relations.

evidence.quote: Arthur H Geissler, Envoy Extraordinary and Minister Plenipotentiary. Appointed: 1922-05-24. Credentials presented: 1922-07-31. Left post on 1930-01-23.

merged_events.event_description: U.S. Legation elevated to Embassy with Boaz Walton Long presenting credentials as the first Ambassador Extraordinary and Plenipotentiary to Guatemala.

evidence.quote: Boaz Walton Long, Ambassador Extraordinary and Plenipotentiary. Appointed: 1943-03-27. Credentials presented: 1943-05-19. Left post on 1945-04-11.

matched.notes: Perfect match. Both record the establishment of the American Legation on 1826-05-03 when Chargé d'Affaires John Williams presented credentials to the Federation of Central American States.

missing_from_csv.event_description: Diplomatic relations established with the Federation of Central American States when President Monroe received Antonio José Cañaz as Envoy Extraordinary and Minister Plenipotentiary. No resident U.S. mission yet established.
missing_from_csv.notes: RDCR high-confidence event. This precedes the first CSV event (1826 Legation) and represents the initial establishment of diplomatic relations via a received foreign envoy, before any U.S. mission was sent.

missing_from_csv.event_description: John Williams made farewell calls, ending the first U.S. mission to the Federation of Central American States. No successor appointed until 1833.
missing_from_csv.notes: POCOM medium-confidence event. Represents a gap in U.S. diplomatic presence after the first Chargé departed.

missing_from_csv.event_description: U.S. mission re-established to the Republic of Central America when Charles DeWitt presented credentials as Chargé d'Affaires.
missing_from_csv.notes: POCOM high-confidence event. Re-establishment of the Legation after the gap following Williams' departure.

missing_from_csv.event_description: Charles DeWitt left post, ending the U.S. mission to the Republic of Central America. No successor appointed until 1848.
missing_from_csv.notes: POCOM medium-confidence event. Date is approximate ('about 1839-01-01'). Represents another gap in U.S. diplomatic presence, coinciding with the dissolution of the Central American federation.

missing_from_csv.event_description: Diplomatic relations re-established with independent Republic of Guatemala when Chargé d'Affaires Elijah Hise presented credentials.
missing_from_csv.notes: RDCR (Event 2, 1849-01-21, 'on or shortly before January 21, 1849') and POCOM (Event 7, 1849-01-31) both cover this event with slightly different dates. Per source priority, RDCR date of 1849-01-21 should be preferred. This is a significant event — the shift from relations with the defunct Central American federation to the independent Republic of Guatemala.

missing_from_csv.event_description: Normal diplomatic relations interrupted when the new government of Guatemala was unrecognized by the United States.
missing_from_csv.notes: POCOM high-confidence event. Represents a break in normal relations due to non-recognition of a new Guatemalan government.

missing_from_csv.event_description: U.S. Legation re-established in Guatemala with Arthur H. Geissler presenting credentials as Envoy Extraordinary and Minister Plenipotentiary, restoring normal diplomatic relations.
missing_from_csv.notes: POCOM high-confidence event. Restoration of normal relations after the 1921 interruption.

missing_from_csv.event_description: U.S. Legation elevated to Embassy when Boaz Walton Long presented credentials as the first Ambassador Extraordinary and Plenipotentiary to Guatemala.
missing_from_csv.notes: POCOM high-confidence event. This is very likely the correct Embassy elevation date. See discrepancy for CSV Row 2.

discrepancies.reasoning: The CSV records the Embassy elevation as 1882-08-10, which matches Event 3 from RDCR. However, Event 3's own evidence is internally contradictory: it claims the Legation was 'raised to Embassy status' on that date, yet states Henry C. Hall's title was 'Envoy Extraordinary and Minister Plenipotentiary' — a title associated with heading a Legation, not an Embassy (which would be headed by an Ambassador). Event 10 from POCOM records Boaz Walton Long as the first Ambassador Extraordinary and Plenipotentiary to Guatemala, presenting credentials on 1943-05-19. This is consistent with the standard pattern of Central American legations being elevated to embassies in the early 1940s. The 1882 date in RDCR likely reflects a credential presentation for a new Minister (a Legation-level event), not an Embassy elevation. The RDCR text appears to contain an error in characterizing this as an Embassy elevation. The correct Embassy elevation date is most likely 1943-05-19. The CSV should be corrected to 1943-05-19 for the Embassy transition.
